_readme: Demo-scale, hand-curated dialect/colloquial term -> scientific entity lexicon.
Per agents/components/dialect-entity-mapping.md, a real deployment must bootstrap this
from low-cost PUBLIC resources (extension-service glossaries, EPPO-style ontologies,
public KGs, published dialect word-lists) rather than new dialect speech collection,
and should be reviewed by a domain/agricultural-extension expert per region before use.
This file is NOT that validated resource -- it is a small, plausible seed lexicon built
from generally well-known North/Central-Indian farmer terminology (the kind that appears
in Krishi Vigyan Kendra / ICAR extension pamphlets) so the MECHANISM (lookup, ambiguity
handling, normalization-before-retrieval) can be exercised end-to-end. Treat every
'maps_to' mapping here as illustrative, not as verified regional linguistics.
Entries with more than one 'maps_to' value are intentionally ambiguous (one colloquial
term covers several scientific entities) -- the mapper surfaces all candidates rather
than guessing, and downstream retrieval is left to disambiguate using the rest of the query.

entries.region_note: Hindi-belt colloquial, generic sap-sucking hopper term
Hindi-belt colloquial variant spelling of deemak
Hindi-belt colloquial ("scorch/blight disease") -- genuinely ambiguous across several blight-type diseases
Hindi-belt colloquial ("rust disease", literal geru=ochre/rust colour)
Hindi-belt colloquial literal translation ("red rot") used for sugarcane
Hindi-belt colloquial ("grain weevil") for storage pests
Bengali colloquial ("leaf twisting/curling") -- ambiguous across leaf-curl diseases
Hindi-belt colloquial ("leaf curling") -- ambiguous, same as pata mora

Hindi-belt colloquial functional request ("fly-killing medicine") rather than a named entity -- maps to the most commonly extension-recommended product class for sucking-pest control, not a literal synonym
Hindi-belt colloquial ("army insect"), widely used after fall armyworm's spread into South Asia
Hindi-belt colloquial ("tobacco caterpillar"-style naming often loosely applied to Helicoverpa/Spodoptera larvae)
Hindi-belt colloquial, loosely used for grain smut/bunt discoloration symptoms
Hindi-belt colloquial ("yellowing leaf disease") loosely applied to virus-yellowing symptoms
